concepts.title: Distribution shift — covariate, label & concept drift

beats.text: Supervised learning assumes production data is drawn from the same distribution the model was trained on.
Write the joint distribution as the product of the conditional and the marginal, and there are three ways it can move.

visual.note: Training fits a fixed P, production feeds a different one

beats.text: Covariate shift moves the inputs, label shift moves the class balance, concept drift moves the relationship itself.

visual.note: Concept drift: the learned function is now wrong, not merely under-sampled

beats.text: Only the first two are visible from inputs alone, so concept drift can hide in the features and surface as collapsing accuracy.
Drift also has a shape in time, classed as sudden, gradual, incremental, or recurring by the Gama taxonomy.
Seasonality is the great impostor, looking like drift but needing only a like-for-like reference window.
Before reacting to drift you have to measure it, and the industry workhorse is the Population Stability Index.
Bin a feature on a reference period, count the same bins live, and sum the weighted log-ratio across buckets.

visual.note: Exactly the symmetrized KL, the Jeffreys divergence

beats.text: The scalar maps onto a stable rule of thumb: below 0.10 is calm, 0.10 to 0.25 is moderate, above 0.25 acts.
One bucket whose mass rises from 0.20 to 0.30 contributes 0.10 times the log of 1.5, about 0.0405.

visual.note: Read the per-bucket breakdown, not just the total

beats.text: Four or five buckets of that size already push the total past the 0.25 alarm threshold.
The same formula on the output score is PSI, on a single input feature it is the Characteristic Stability Index.
PSI is a batch statistic over a window, but production also wants streaming detectors that flag a change at once.
Labels arrive late, so the first line of defence is two-sample tests on the feature distribution.
Label-free detection can only ever see covariate shift, staying silent while pure concept drift rots accuracy.
The classic error monitor is DDM, tracking the running error rate and its standard deviation over time.

visual.note: Remember the best point reached and watch for climbs above it

beats.text: DDM warns at two sigma above the best error and declares drift at three, the control-chart logic on a learning curve.

visual.note: Two sigma warns, three sigma declares drift

beats.text: A sensitive detector catches drift early but cries wolf, so you tune the operating point to the cost of each error.
Detection is half the loop; a monitor must turn a signal into a decision to wait, alert, or retrain.
Three trigger philosophies exist: scheduled cadence, performance thresholds on labels, and label-free drift statistics.
Drift triggers are the early warning while labels are in flight; performance triggers are the authoritative one.
Retrain too often and you waste compute; too rarely and you eat decay, so the optimum balances both terms.

visual.note: Amortized retrain falls with T, carried decay rises with T

beats.text: Setting the derivative to zero gives a square-root cadence, the same shape as the economic-order-quantity rule.

visual.note: Cost 200, decay 1 per day gives 20 days

beats.text: With a retrain cost of 200 and decay of one unit per day, the optimal interval is twenty days.

concepts.title: A model decaying in the wild

beats.text: A deployed model performs near its validation score, holds, then bends downward as the world drifts away.

visual.text: A model decaying in the wild

beats.text: Between retrains accuracy falls roughly linearly from its ceiling at rate d, buried in measurement noise.

visual.note: A retrain resets the clock and lifts performance back to the ceiling

beats.text: Trace it over many cycles and you get the familiar sawtooth of decay and snap-back recovery.
The area between the ceiling and the sawtooth is the value lost to drift, shrunk by retraining more often.
In the simulator a thirty-day cadence recovers measurable mean accuracy over a model that never retrains.
Drift monitoring tells you that the model changed; the next chapter on explainability tells you why.

visual.note: Faster drift tightens the cadence, costlier retrains loosen it
